ing emanating from the auspicious quarters (east, north and north-
east), a wise astrologer should predict very good growth of all
crops. If there be rain with dust-stOrm, or if children be engaged
in happy sports, the cries of birds be pleasant and their sport be in
dust, water and the like, if the luminaries be surrounded by halos
that are glossy and not broken, then too there would be good rain
helping the growth of all crops. If the clouds be glossy, collected
together and moving in a clockwise manner, there would be heavy
rains tending to the growth of all crops.
[These five verses are quoted by our author from the work of
Sage Vasistha. Most of the ancient sages wrote in the Sloka
metre.]

Chapter XXUI—Rainfall

Should there be rain in the lunar mansions beginning with
Purva^aqlha after the full Moon in the month of Jye§tha, astrologers
should judge the quantity of rainfall as well as the good and bad
effects thereof.
